explanation_md: `std::count` finds two 3s in the vector. After `std::sort`, the vector becomes `{1,3,3,5,8,9}`. `std::find` for 8 returns an iterator at index 4 (0-based). So the output is `2` then `4`.

prompt_md: What does `std::find(v.begin(), v.end(), 42)` return if the value 42 is not present in vector `v`?

options.b: `v.begin()` — indicating failure by resetting to the start.
options.d: An iterator pointing at the last element checked.

explanation_md: `std::find` returns the end iterator when the value is not found. This is the standard sentinel convention for all range-based search algorithms. You must check `it != v.end()` before dereferencing the result.

options.a: `*it` dereferences the iterator to get the value, not the index; the index is `it - v.begin()`.
options.b: The result of `std::find` must be cast to `int` before printing.
options.c: `std::find` returns an index, not an iterator, so `*it` does not compile.
options.d: The vector must be sorted before calling `std::find`.

explanation_md: `std::find` returns an iterator. Dereferencing with `*it` gives the element value (7), not the position. To get the 0-based index, compute `it - v.begin()`. (Also, this code does not check `it != v.end()` first, which would be undefined behaviour if 7 were absent.)

prompt_md: Why does `std::sort(lst.begin(), lst.end())` fail to compile when `lst` is a `std::list<int>`?

options.a: `std::sort` requires random-access iterators; `std::list` iterators only support `++` and `--`.
options.b: `std::sort` only works on `std::vector`, not any other container.
options.c: `std::list` does not have `begin()` and `end()` member functions.
options.d: You must pass the size of the list as a third argument to `std::sort`.

explanation_md: `std::sort` needs to jump to arbitrary positions in O(1), which requires random-access iterators. `std::list` provides only bidirectional iterators (forward and backward, one step at a time). The standard library provides `std::list::sort()` as a member function that works with the linked structure directly.
